instruction: How do I use perf stat to measure cache misses and understand if my C++ code is memory-bound or compute-bound?
output:
<think>
perf stat reads hardware performance counters to show cache misses, branch mispredictions, instructions per cycle (IPC), and other microarchitectural metrics. This tells you whether code is bottlenecked on compute (low IPC due to dependencies), memory (high cache miss rate), or branches (high misprediction rate).

Key metrics: (1) IPC (instructions per cycle) — modern CPUs can do 3-5 IPC. Below 1 = stalled (memory or branch bound). (2) L1/L2/LLC cache miss rate — high miss rate means data access patterns are bad. (3) Branch misprediction rate — above 5% is problematic, consider branchless code.

For memory-bound code, the fix is improving data locality: use arrays instead of linked lists, pack structs, prefetch, and process data in cache-line sized chunks. For branch-bound code: use branchless techniques, CMOV, or sort data to make branches predictable.

The command is: `perf stat -e cache-misses,cache-references,L1-dcache-load-misses,LLC-load-misses,branch-misses,instructions,cycles ./my_program`. Run multiple times and look at percentages, not absolute numbers.
</think>

```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <random>
#include <algorithm>
#include <iostream>
#include <chrono>

// Demonstrate memory-bound vs compute-bound patterns

// MEMORY-BOUND: random access pattern causes cache misses
void random_access(const std::vector<int>& data, const std::vector<int>& indices) {
    volatile long sum = 0;
    for (int idx : indices) {
        sum += data[idx];  // random access = L1/L2 cache misses
    }
}

// COMPUTE-BOUND: sequential access, high IPC
void sequential_access(const std::vector<int>& data) {
    volatile long sum = 0;
    for (int val : data) {
        sum += val;  // sequential = prefetcher-friendly, cache hits
    }
}

// BRANCH-BOUND: unpredictable branches
void branchy_code(const std::vector<int>& data) {
    volatile long sum = 0;
    for (int val : data) {
        if (val > 500) {  // 50% taken = worst case for predictor
            sum += val;
        } else {
            sum -= val;
        }
    }
}

// BRANCHLESS: same logic, no branch mispredictions
void branchless_code(const std::vector<int>& data) {
    volatile long sum = 0;
    for (int val : data) {
        int mask = -(val > 500); // branchless: 0 or -1
        sum += (val & mask) - (val & ~mask);
    }
}

int main() {
    const int N = 10'000'000;
    std::mt19937 rng(42);

    std::vector<int> data(N);
    std::generate(data.begin(), data.end(), [&]{ return rng() % 1000; });

    // Random indices for cache-miss demonstration
    std::vector<int> indices(N);
    std::generate(indices.begin(), indices.end(), [&]{ return rng() % N; });

    auto bench = [](const char* name, auto fn) {
        auto start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        fn();
        auto end = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        auto ms = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::milliseconds>(end - start).count();
        std::cout << name << ": " << ms << " ms\n";
    };

    bench("random_access",    [&]{ random_access(data, indices); });
    bench("sequential_access", [&]{ sequential_access(data); });
    bench("branchy_code",     [&]{ branchy_code(data); });
    bench("branchless_code",  [&]{ branchless_code(data); });

    return 0;
}

// === Compile and profile ===
// g++ -O2 -o bench bench.cpp
// perf stat -e cycles,instructions,cache-misses,cache-references,\n//   L1-dcache-load-misses,branch-misses,branches ./bench
//
// Expected results:
//   random_access:    HIGH cache-misses (>10%), IPC < 1.0
//   sequential_access: LOW cache-misses (<1%), IPC > 2.0
//   branchy_code:     HIGH branch-misses (>10%)
//   branchless_code:  LOW branch-misses (<1%)
```

instruction: How do I use VTune or similar tools to find hotspots in C++ code and understand the microarchitectural bottlenecks?
output:
<think>
Intel VTune Profiler is the most detailed CPU profiler for x86. It uses hardware performance monitoring units (PMUs) to show exactly why code is slow at the instruction level. The key analysis types:

1. Hotspots — where CPU time is spent (like perf top)
2. Microarchitecture Exploration — categorizes stalls into: front-end bound, back-end bound (memory or core), bad speculation (branch misprediction), retiring (actual useful work)
3. Memory Access — tracks cache hierarchy usage per memory access
4. Threading — identifies lock contention, load imbalance

The Top-Down Microarchitecture Analysis (TMA) method breaks CPU time into: Retiring (good work), Bad Speculation (wasted), Front-End Bound (instruction fetch/decode stalls), Back-End Bound (execution stalls subdivided into memory and core).

Free alternatives: perf + toplev (pmu-tools), AMD uProf for AMD CPUs. The concepts are identical.

For practical optimization: if Back-End Memory Bound is high, improve data locality. If Bad Speculation is high, reduce branches. If Front-End Bound is high, reduce code size (fewer templates/inlining).
</think>

```cpp
// === VTune workflow ===
// 1. Compile with debug info: g++ -O2 -g -o app app.cpp
// 2. Run VTune: vtune -collect hotspots ./app
// 3. View results: vtune-gui r000hs/  (or vtune -report hotspots -r r000hs)

// === Alternative: perf + toplev (free, open-source) ===
// pip install pmu-tools
// toplev -l2 --no-desc -- ./app
// Output:
//   FE             Frontend_Bound:   15.2%
//   BE             Backend_Bound:    62.3%  <<<< main bottleneck
//   BE/Mem         Backend_Bound.Memory_Bound:  48.1%
//   BE/Core        Backend_Bound.Core_Bound:    14.2%
//   BAD            Bad_Speculation:  8.5%
//   RET            Retiring:         14.0%

#include <vector>
#include <cmath>
#include <iostream>
#include <numeric>

// Example code with different bottleneck types:

// FRONT-END BOUND: excessive template instantiation / code bloat
template<int N>
struct HeavyTemplate {
    double compute(double x) {
        return std::sin(x * N) + std::cos(x * N) +
               std::exp(x / N) + std::log(x + N);
    }
};

// BACK-END MEMORY BOUND: poor data layout
struct AoS {  // Array of Structs — bad cache utilization
    double x, y, z;
    char padding[256];  // large struct, wastes cache lines
};

void process_aos(std::vector<AoS>& data) {
    for (auto& item : data) {
        item.x = std::sqrt(item.x * item.x + item.y * item.y);
    }
}

// GOOD: Struct of Arrays — cache-friendly
struct SoA {
    std::vector<double> x, y, z;  // contiguous per-field arrays
};

void process_soa(SoA& data) {
    for (size_t i = 0; i < data.x.size(); ++i) {
        data.x[i] = std::sqrt(data.x[i] * data.x[i] + data.y[i] * data.y[i]);
    }
}

// BACK-END CORE BOUND: long dependency chains
double serial_dependency(const std::vector<double>& data) {
    double result = 0.0;
    for (double x : data) {
        result = std::sin(result + x);  // each iteration depends on previous
    }
    return result;
}

// GOOD: break dependency chain (ILP-friendly)
double parallel_reduction(const std::vector<double>& data) {
    double sum0 = 0, sum1 = 0, sum2 = 0, sum3 = 0;
    size_t i = 0;
    for (; i + 3 < data.size(); i += 4) {
        sum0 += data[i];
        sum1 += data[i+1];
        sum2 += data[i+2];
        sum3 += data[i+3];
    }
    for (; i < data.size(); ++i) sum0 += data[i];
    return sum0 + sum1 + sum2 + sum3;
}

int main() {
    const size_t N = 1'000'000;
    std::vector<double> data(N, 1.0);

    // Profile each function separately to isolate bottleneck type
    volatile double r1 = serial_dependency(data);
    volatile double r2 = parallel_reduction(data);

    std::cout << r1 << " " << r2 << std::endl;
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: How do I profile branch prediction in my C++ code and write branchless alternatives for hot paths?
output:
<think>
Branch misprediction costs 15-20 cycles on modern CPUs (the entire pipeline is flushed). For code running billions of times, even 5% misprediction rate is devastating. The CPU's branch predictor works well for predictable patterns (always taken, always not taken, repeating pattern) but fails on random data-dependent branches.

To measure: `perf stat -e branch-misses,branches ./app` gives the misprediction rate. `perf annotate` shows per-instruction branch miss counts. `perf record -e branch-misses ./app` samples only mispredictions.

Branchless techniques: (1) conditional move (CMOV) — the compiler generates this with `-O2` for simple ternaries, but not always. (2) Arithmetic: `(val > threshold)` returns 0 or 1, use it as a multiplier. (3) Bitmask: `-condition` gives 0x0 or 0xFF..F, AND with value. (4) `std::min`/`std::max` — often compiled to CMOV. (5) Lookup tables for small enum-based dispatch.

Caveat: branchless code is NOT always faster. If the branch is predictable (>95% one direction), the branch is nearly free and branchless adds instruction overhead. Only optimize branches with high misprediction rates.
</think>

```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <random>
#include <chrono>
#include <iostream>
#include <cstring>

// === Measuring branch misprediction ===
// perf stat -e branch-misses,branches,branch-instructions ./app

// BRANCHY: data-dependent, unpredictable
int clamp_branchy(int val, int lo, int hi) {
    if (val < lo) return lo;      // unpredictable if val is random
    if (val > hi) return hi;
    return val;
}

// BRANCHLESS: std::clamp uses CMOV on x86 with -O2
int clamp_branchless(int val, int lo, int hi) {
    return std::clamp(val, lo, hi); // compiler generates CMOV
}

// BRANCHY: absolute value with branch
int abs_branchy(int x) {
    return x < 0 ? -x : x;  // branch on sign
}

// BRANCHLESS: arithmetic absolute value
int abs_branchless(int x) {
    int mask = x >> 31;       // 0x0 if positive, 0xFFFFFFFF if negative
    return (x ^ mask) - mask; // flips bits + adds 1 if negative
}

// BRANCHY: conditional accumulation
long sum_positive_branchy(const std::vector<int>& data) {
    long sum = 0;
    for (int val : data) {
        if (val > 0) {  // 50% random = worst case branch prediction
            sum += val;
        }
    }
    return sum;
}

// BRANCHLESS: mask-based conditional accumulation
long sum_positive_branchless(const std::vector<int>& data) {
    long sum = 0;
    for (int val : data) {
        // (val > 0) is 0 or 1; multiply to conditionally add
        sum += val * (val > 0);
    }
    return sum;
}

// SORTED DATA: branch becomes predictable
long sum_positive_sorted(std::vector<int> data) {
    std::sort(data.begin(), data.end());
    long sum = 0;
    for (int val : data) {
        if (val > 0) sum += val;  // predictable after sort!
    }
    return sum;
}

int main() {
    const int N = 50'000'000;
    std::mt19937 rng(42);
    std::uniform_int_distribution<int> dist(-1000, 1000);

    std::vector<int> data(N);
    std::generate(data.begin(), data.end(), [&]{ return dist(rng); });

    auto bench = [](const char* name, auto fn) {
        auto start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        volatile auto result = fn();
        auto ms = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::milliseconds>(
            std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now() - start).count();
        std::cout << name << ": " << ms << " ms\n";
    };

    bench("branchy",    [&]{ return sum_positive_branchy(data); });
    bench("branchless", [&]{ return sum_positive_branchless(data); });
    bench("sorted",     [&]{ return sum_positive_sorted(data); });

    return 0;
}

// Compile: g++ -O2 -march=native -o branch bench.cpp
// Profile: perf stat -e branch-misses,branches ./branch
//
// Expected:
//   branchy:    ~150ms, branch-misses ~25%
//   branchless: ~80ms,  branch-misses ~0%
//   sorted:     ~100ms, branch-misses ~0% (predictor learns pattern)
```

instruction: How do I analyze cache behavior in my C++ code to optimize data layouts for better performance?
output:
<think>
Cache optimization is the single biggest performance lever in modern C++. L1 cache access: ~4 cycles. L2: ~12 cycles. L3: ~40 cycles. Main memory: ~200 cycles. A cache miss costs 50x more than a hit.

To analyze cache behavior: `perf stat -e L1-dcache-load-misses,L1-dcache-loads,LLC-load-misses,LLC-loads ./app`. The miss rate tells you if your data access pattern is cache-friendly.

For detailed per-function analysis: `perf record -e cache-misses ./app` then `perf annotate` to see which loads are missing.

Optimization techniques: (1) Struct of Arrays (SoA) instead of Array of Structs (AoS) when you only access some fields. (2) Struct packing to fit hot fields in one cache line (64 bytes). (3) Data-oriented design: organize by access pattern, not by object hierarchy. (4) Prefetching with `__builtin_prefetch` for predictable access patterns. (5) Avoid pointer chasing (linked lists, trees) — use contiguous arrays with indices.

The cachegrind tool (Valgrind) simulates the cache hierarchy and shows exact miss counts per line, but it's 20-50x slower than native execution.
</think>

```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <chrono>
#include <iostream>
#include <cstring>

// === Data layout comparison: AoS vs SoA ===

// Array of Structs: bad for field-only access
struct Particle_AoS {
    float x, y, z;          // position (12 bytes, used)
    float vx, vy, vz;       // velocity (12 bytes, used)
    float mass;              // 4 bytes, NOT used in update
    char  name[32];          // 32 bytes, NOT used in update
    int   id;                // 4 bytes, NOT used in update
    // Total: 64 bytes per particle, but update only needs 24 bytes
    // Each cache line (64B) holds 1 particle, wastes 40 bytes
};

void update_aos(std::vector<Particle_AoS>& particles, float dt) {
    for (auto& p : particles) {
        p.x += p.vx * dt;  // loads 64B cache line, uses 8 bytes
        p.y += p.vy * dt;
        p.z += p.vz * dt;
    }
}

// Struct of Arrays: cache-optimal for field-only access
struct Particles_SoA {
    std::vector<float> x, y, z;      // positions (contiguous)
    std::vector<float> vx, vy, vz;   // velocities (contiguous)
    std::vector<float> mass;
    std::vector<int> id;
    size_t count;
};

void update_soa(Particles_SoA& p, float dt) {
    for (size_t i = 0; i < p.count; ++i) {
        p.x[i] += p.vx[i] * dt;  // sequential float access = perfect prefetch
        p.y[i] += p.vy[i] * dt;
        p.z[i] += p.vz[i] * dt;
    }
}

// === Hot/cold splitting: keep hot data in one cache line ===
struct Entity_Bad {
    float x, y, z;      // hot: accessed every frame
    float health;        // hot
    char  name[128];     // cold: accessed rarely
    int   inventory[64]; // cold: accessed rarely
    // Hot and cold data mixed = cache lines wasted on cold data
};

struct Entity_Hot {
    float x, y, z;
    float health;
    int cold_index;  // index into cold storage
};

struct Entity_Cold {
    char  name[128];
    int   inventory[64];
};

// Hot data fits in 20 bytes; 3 entities per cache line instead of 0

int main() {
    const int N = 10'000'000;
    float dt = 0.016f;

    // AoS setup
    std::vector<Particle_AoS> aos(N);
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        aos[i] = {(float)i, (float)i, (float)i, 1,1,1, 1.0f, "", i};
    }

    // SoA setup
    Particles_SoA soa;
    soa.count = N;
    soa.x.resize(N); soa.y.resize(N); soa.z.resize(N);
    soa.vx.assign(N, 1.0f); soa.vy.assign(N, 1.0f); soa.vz.assign(N, 1.0f);

    auto bench = [](const char* name, auto fn) {
        auto start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        fn();
        auto ms = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::milliseconds>(
            std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now() - start).count();
        std::cout << name << ": " << ms << " ms\n";
    };

    bench("AoS update", [&]{ update_aos(aos, dt); });
    bench("SoA update", [&]{ update_soa(soa, dt); });
    // SoA is typically 3-5x faster due to cache efficiency

    return 0;
}

// Profile: perf stat -e L1-dcache-load-misses,L1-dcache-loads ./cache_bench
// AoS: ~40% L1 miss rate (loads unused fields)
// SoA: ~2% L1 miss rate (sequential float access)
```
